refs.note: "as you learned in the first hearing": back-reference to the actio prima's listing of the Sicilian charges already proved by witnesses.
Refers back to Verres's confession in the actio prima that the pirate captains had not been struck with the axe.

"I said in the first hearing that I would make plain that Gaius Verres had taken from Sicily forty million sesterces" --- explicit programmatic crossref to the actio prima's headline charge, the figure that orients Books 2-3 (the praetorship) and 4-5 (the plunder of Sicily).
"I have done all things in the previous hearing in such a way that on no charge was there any in which any of you needed a continuous prosecution" --- methodological self-reference.
"You heard Charidemus of Chios speak in evidence in the previous hearing": refers to Charidemus's testimony at the actio prima.
"in Tettius's testimony in the previous hearing": Verres's interruption of P. Tettius at the actio prima.
"although you have learned it from many cities of Sicily in the previous hearing": Verres's pattern of expunging public records, anticipated through the actio prima's Sicilian witnesses.
"hear what you learned in the previous hearing": M. Octavius Ligus's evidence.
"You, gentlemen, learned them earlier": appeal to the jurors' memory of the actio prima.
"in the previous hearing Quintus Hortensius complained": Hortensius's complaint about the ward Iunius being produced in the praetexta.
"what the witnesses from Sicily say": looks ahead to Books 2-2 to 2-5, the Sicilian portion of the prosecution.

The "subsortition of jurors of Iunius" is the same scandal Cicero will later defend Cluentius from, eight years later, in 66 BC: the trial of Oppianicus before C. Iunius the quaesitor, in which Cicero alleges Verres forged the jury album.
